findings.description: SQL injection: the login query is built with an f-string interpolating the attacker-controlled email, so a payload like ' OR '1'='1' -- bypasses authentication (CWE-89 / OWASP A03); fix with a parameterized query.

Inverted session-expiry check: 'if session.expires_at > now' deletes/rejects still-valid sessions and lets EXPIRED sessions fall through to 'return session.user_id', so expired tokens are honored indefinitely (CWE-613); the comparison must be '<=' (scanner missed this).

Permanent-lockout DoS: LOCKOUT_MINUTES is never used and failed_attempts only resets on a successful login (impossible while locked), so 5 bad attempts against a known email lock the victim out forever (CWE-645).

Username enumeration / timing oracle: unknown emails return early before the 100k-iteration PBKDF2 hash runs while valid emails incur the full hash, exposing a timing and response-path discrepancy (CWE-203/CWE-208); perform a dummy hash for unknown users.

find_user_by_email runs 'SELECT * FROM users' and filters in Python, loading every user's password_hash and salt into memory on each lookup — needless credential blast-radius and O(n) cost; query by email with a parameterized WHERE clause instead.

Scanner-flagged 'log injection': user.id is logged via lazy %s formatting and is an int from the DB, so there is no realistic CRLF/log-forging vector here — assessed as a likely false positive, reported only for exhaustiveness.
Scanner-flagged 'log injection': session.user_id is an int logged via lazy %s formatting, so no attacker-controlled string reaches the log — likely false positive, reported only for exhaustiveness.

datetime.utcnow() is deprecated (3.12+) and returns naive datetimes, making the session-expiry comparisons timezone-fragile; not a vulnerability itself but compounds the expiry-logic risk — use datetime.now(timezone.utc).
